hero.sub: I build production workflows in `n8n` and `Python` for marketing and operations — idempotent by design, GDPR-aware by default, and never silently failing. Five years across digital marketing, paid acquisition, and systems security.

metrics.label: attribution data recovered after iOS 14+ via server-side Meta CAPI integration
blended ROAS on student-enrolment campaigns (£3–4k/mo spend → ~£10k/mo commission)
cost per enrolment against £2,000 conversion value — profitable acquisition by design
duplicate conversions possible — deterministic event IDs make every retry idempotent

projects.title: Meta Conversions API — server-side tracking
projects.description: Production CAPI implementation that recovers conversions lost to iOS privacy changes — built around the two problems example code ignores: double counting and PII custody.

decisions.text: deterministic event IDs — Pixel + server dedupe, retries can't double-count
hash-at-the-boundary — plaintext PII exists in exactly one audited function
exponential backoff on rate limits · PII-safe logging · 10/10 tests passing

projects.title: n8n lead pipeline — capture to CRM
projects.description: Lead intake that's trustworthy, not just automatic: validation and consent gating at the door, idempotent CRM writes, and failures that alert a human instead of vanishing.

decisions.text: reject bad data with a 400 before it ever touches the CRM
GDPR consent enforced in the pipeline, timestamp stored for audit
error branch alerts Slack with a traceable enquiry ID for replay

principles.body: Deterministic IDs, upserts keyed on stable references — a double-submitted form or a network retry can never create a duplicate contact or inflate a revenue metric.

principles.lead: PII is hashed where it enters.
principles.body: Customer data is normalised and SHA-256 hashed in one audited place; downstream code, payloads, and logs never see plaintext. Data minimisation as architecture, not policy.
Enrichment may fail quietly — a lead without enrichment beats a lost lead. The CRM write may not. Knowing which is which is most of the design work.
Eight years close to revenue — business development, paid acquisition, lead ops — means I build the workflow that moves the P&L, not the one that demos well.

paragraphs: Most automation specialists learned the tools first and the business later. I did it the other way around — and it shows in everything I ship.

I spent three years in Dhaka closing ~£90,000 design contracts before I ever wrote a workflow, then moved to London and ran paid acquisition where every pound had to defend itself: ~3× blended ROAS, £700 cost per enrolment against £2,000 of value. So when iOS 14 quietly erased a fifth of our conversion data, I didn't raise a ticket and wait. I learned Meta's Conversions API, wrote the Python integration myself, and got our attribution back. That project changed my career — it's where "marketer who uses automation" became "engineer who understands marketing."

My MSc in Computer Network & System Security is the other half of the story. Automation touches the two things a business can least afford to get wrong — money and customer data — so I build GDPR compliance, secure key handling and auditability into the architecture, not the documentation. The proof is public: both repositories below are tested, documented around their design decisions, and built the way I'd build them for production. Because they were.

The part that doesn't show up in a repo: I've trained sceptical, non-technical teams to actually adopt this stuff. Automation that the team works around is just expensive shelf-ware — I build systems people trust, and I bring them along.
